result.markdown:
# R is Friend

::: callout
## Learning Objectives

+ Identify three of the foundational data types in R
+ Explain similarities and differences between different object types
+ Use indexing to extract elements from different object types
+ Use functions to calculate summary statistics
+ Use logical values and indexing to calculate summary statistics
:::

## Data Types

In the last class, you learned how to create simple objects (e.g., `x <- 1221`, 
`name <- "jane"`), but we never talked about the difference between a value 
like `1221` and `"jane"`. These two values have different **data types**---one
is a **number** and one is a **character** (really, a set of characters). These 
are two of what are called the ["atomic" data types in R](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-lang.html#Vectors). Let's 
dig into these data types more!

### Numeric Values

You've seen a lot of numbers in your day, so it probably wouldn't surprise you
to know that there are different types of numbers. There are numbers **with**
decimals and there are numbers **without** decimals. Technically, R separates
these two types of numbers into their own data types---**doubles** and
**integers**.

#### Double (Real) Values

If a number has a decimal value (e.g., `26.2`), then R will store the number 
as a double (real) numeric. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
distance <- 26.2
typeof(distance)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "double"
```


:::
:::


::: {.callout-tip}
# `typeof()`

The `typeof()` function tells you the storage type that R is using internally to
hold the data. You can think of this function asking the question, 
"How is this data physically stored in memory?". 
:::

#### Integer Values

Numbers that **do not** have decimals are called `integer`s. If you want a number
to be stored as an `integer`, you need to tell R to do so. Otherwise, your
number will still be stored as a `double`.

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

Defaults to `double`:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
room <- 1221
typeof(room)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "double"
```


:::
:::


:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
Adding an `L` to the end creates an `integer`:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
room <- 1221L
typeof(room)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "integer"
```


:::
:::

:::
:::

::: {.callout-opinion}

You might wonder why `L` is used to create an `integer`. Wouldn't an `I` make 
more sense?

Because R is written mostly in C (another programming language), it likely
inherits some naming conventions from C. In C, `L` is used to denote a (long)
integer. Moreover, it would be confusing to use an `I` to indicate an `integer`
since you use a `"i"` suffix to create a complex number. 

:::

::: {.callout-tip}
# Other ways to create an `integer`

Adding an `L` at the end of a number is one way to make an integer, but this
really only works when we are creating our objects by hand. More frequently, 
we will be given a vector of numbers which we may want to transform into 
integer values. The `as.integer()` function can help us do just that!


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
room <- 1410
room <- as.integer(room)
typeof(room)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "integer"
```


:::
:::

:::

<!-- We could choose to highlight how `class()` returns the same value (numeric) for -->
<!-- both doubles and integers.  -->

<!-- class() tells you the high-level type that R uses to decide how to behave with -->
<!-- the object. Think of it as asking: "What role does this object play?"  -->

<!-- Imagine a chef's knife: -->
<!-- typeof() → "It's made of stainless steel" (the raw material) -->
<!-- class() → "It's a kitchen tool" (the role it plays) -->

### Character Values

A `character` value is somewhat the opposite of a number in that it contains 
qualitative information. In R, the `character` data type represents objects 
that can have any number of characters (e.g., `"a"`, `"ab"`, `"abc"`). A 
`character` data type can be identified by the use of quotation marks (`""`)
surrounding the values. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
first_name <- "lilly"
typeof(first_name)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "character"
```


:::
:::


::: {.callout-tip}
# Converting a number into a `character`

Converting between data types is something we will frequently find ourselves 
doing. Time related variables (e.g., `year`, `month`) are variables that are 
typically read in as numeric but we are often interested in using them as a
character. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
year <- 1988
as.character(year)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "1988"
```


:::
:::


Notice the number now has quotations around it!
:::

### Logical Values

The last data type is one we haven't seen before. Logicals have two possible 
values---`TRUE` and `FALSE`. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
passed <- TRUE
typeof(passed)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "logical"
```


:::
:::


::: {.callout-tip}
# Relationship with Numeric Values

Logical values have binary numerical representations.

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}
A `TRUE` is represented by a `1`.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
as.numeric(TRUE)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 1
```


:::
:::


:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
A `FALSE` is represented by a `0`. 

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
as.numeric(FALSE)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 0
```


:::
:::

:::
:::
:::

### Other Data Types

So far, we've covered real (double), integer, character, and logical data types.
These form all but two of the atomic data types. The only two that remain are
**complex** and **raw**. As you might expect from its name, the complex data
type is used to store complex numbers (e.g., `4 + 3i`). The raw data type is 
possibly the least used data type, whose purpose is to store data at the byte
level.

In STAT 1810 and 3820 we will **only** work with real, integer, character, and
logical data types!

## Object Types
<!-- Intro to vectors, lists, matrices, dataframes, tibbles.  -->
<!-- Building up objects -->
<!-- Small object (from last week) is a scalar value -->
<!-- Bigger object is a vector (multiple values) -->
<!-- What if we have multiple vectors? -->
<!-- To store them in a matrix, they all need to be the same data type. -->
<!-- If they are different data types, then we need a list.  -->
<!-- What if the vectors are all the same length? -->
<!-- Then we can use a data frame (or a tibble)! -->

Now that we've learned about the different types of data values, let's learn
about the different ways data could be stored. 

<!-- Give basic info / examples. -->
<!-- How to create each type of structure. -->

### Vectors

You encountered a vector in your last lab assignment! Vectors are created using
the `c()` function (which stands for concatenate). Vectors have a few important
qualities:

- They have **one** dimension, their length. 
- Their entries must all have the **same** data type. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
names <- c("Jim", "Weun", "Pat", "Pran", "Phupha", "Tian")
length(names)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 6
```


:::
:::


In the last lab, you added a character to a vector of numbers. Even though the
objects had different data types, R didn't output an error message. Indeed, it
concatenated the objects together into one vector. But what data type was the
resulting vector?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
c(67, 13, 1989, "hello")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "67"    "13"    "1989"  "hello"
```


:::
:::


It made all the numbers into characters! This is called **data type coercion**, 
where R changes / coerces the data type of elements of the vector so they are
all the same data type. 

::: {.callout-check-in}

For each of the examples below, identify what data type do you believe the
vector will have.

1. `c(67, 13, 1989, TRUE)`
2. `c("hello", "goodbye", TRUE)`
3. `c("TRUE", FALSE, TRUE, "FALSE")`
:::

### Multiple Vectors

When working with data, we will find ourselves with *multiple* vectors---one for
each variable that was recorded. When assembling multiple vectors into an object
the key question is whether the vectors all have the **same** data type or 
**different** data types.

#### Do all of the vectors have the same data type?

Vectors that all have the **same** data type can be made into a matrix. A matrix
has **two** dimensions---rows and columns. We can create a matrix of values from
a vector by specifying the number of rows (`nrow`) or the number of columns 
(`ncol`). 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
names <- c("Jim", "Weun", "Pat", "Pran", "Phupha", "Tian")
```
:::


::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

Specifying the number of rows (`nrow`):


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
matrix(names, nrow = 3)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1]   [,2]    
[1,] "Jim"  "Pran"  
[2,] "Weun" "Phupha"
[3,] "Pat"  "Tian"  
```


:::
:::

:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

Specifying the number of columns (`ncol`):


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
matrix(names, ncol = 3)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1]   [,2]   [,3]    
[1,] "Jim"  "Pat"  "Phupha"
[2,] "Weun" "Pran" "Tian"  
```


:::
:::

:::
:::

The vector above has length 6, which is divisible by 3. What do
you think will happen if the length of the vector isn't divisible by the number
of rows?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
new_names <- c("Jim", "Weun", "Pat", "Pran", "Phupha", "Black", "Sean")

matrix(new_names, ncol = 3)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in matrix(new_names, ncol = 3): data length [7] is not a sub-multiple
or multiple of the number of rows [3]
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1]   [,2]     [,3]  
[1,] "Jim"  "Pran"   "Sean"
[2,] "Weun" "Phupha" "Jim" 
[3,] "Pat"  "Black"  "Weun"
```


:::
:::

First, R gives a warning message telling us that the length of the vector 
(7) is not a multiple of the number of rows we input (`3`). 
Then, it **recycles** the first value of the vector (Jim) to fill
the spot where there wasn't a value. 

This can produce misleading data, so we should **always** make sure the length
of our vector is a multiple of the dimension of the matrix (e.g., rows, columns)
we are trying to make. 

::: {.callout-check-in}
4. Draw out how this matrix will look:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", 
           "Bad Buddy", 
           "Moonlight Chicken", 
           "Not Me")

matrix(shows, ncol = 2)
```
:::

:::

::: {.callout-opinion}
You might have noticed that the formatting of `shows` is a bit different from
what we used before. Why is that?

One of the formatting guidelines for writing code is that your code should not
be more than 80 characters long. This line of code is 82 characters long:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", "Bad Buddy", "Moonlight Chicken", "Not Me")
```
:::


Since it is longer than 80 characters, we need to break up the code. Meaning, we
need to use a new line (return) somewhere in our code. There are few different 
ways we could have done this:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", "Bad Buddy", "Moonlight Chicken", 
           "Not Me")

shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", "Bad Buddy", 
           "Moonlight Chicken", "Not Me")
```
:::


But, to us, these make it harder to see the length of the vector. So, that is 
why we chose to use a new line for every element of the vector. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", 
           "Bad Buddy", 
           "Moonlight Chicken", 
           "Not Me")
```
:::


:::

#### Do the vectors have different data types?

What if we wanted to combine vectors that have **different** data types? Since 
a matrix requires all the entries to be the same data type, we will need to find
a new solution! 

A **list** is an object that allows entries to be different data types. Suppose
for each show we have its release date. The name of the show is a `character` 
and the release date of the show is `numeric`. We can use a list to combine 
these two vectors into one object (`top_bl`). 




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
release_years <- c(2021, 2021, 2023, 2022)

top_bl <- list(shows, release_years)
top_bl
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[[1]]
[1] "Tale of a Thousand Stars" "Bad Buddy"               
[3] "Moonlight Chicken"        "Not Me"                  

[[2]]
[1] 2021 2021 2023 2022
```


:::
:::

The output of a list is a bit unexpected, so let's break it down. The `[[]]` 
represents the number of the component. So, `[[1]]` represents the `shows` and
`[[2]]` represents the `release_years`. The `[]` represents the number of the
item *within* each component. So, `"Tale of a Thousand Stars"` is the first
(`[1]`) item of the first component (`[[1]]`). 

We find it easier to think of lists as cubby bins. Each `[[]]` represents a bin
and the `[]` represent the items in the bin. Within a bin the objects are the 
**same** data type, but each bin can have different types of objects. 

![A `list` analogy with a cubby bin. Each cubby has different types of items (e.g., puzzles, dolls, legos) but within a cubby the items are all the same.](images/02-list-cubbies.jpg){fig-alt="A picture of a wood cubby bin with six different cubby slots. Each cubby is a different color (red, yellow, blue, green, purple, orange) and each cubby contains different types of objects (legos, books, cars, paints, dolls). However, within a cubby bin the items are all the same. Meaning, the bin of dolls does not also contain legos." width=50%}

We could have modified our method for creating the list by giving each component
a name. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
top_bl <- list(show_name = shows, release_year = release_years)
top_bl
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$show_name
[1] "Tale of a Thousand Stars" "Bad Buddy"               
[3] "Moonlight Chicken"        "Not Me"                  

$release_year
[1] 2021 2021 2023 2022
```


:::
:::


Notice now there are `$` symbols instead of `[[]]` symbols. The `$` in
front of `show_name` and `release_year` represents the **name** of that 
component. We technically call this a *named list* since every component has a
name.

![A `list` analogy with a cubby bin. Similar to before, each cubby has different types of items (e.g., puzzles, dolls, legos) but within a cubby the items are all the same. However, now the cubbies all have names identifying the contents of the cubby.](images/02-lists-labeled-cubbies.png){fig-alt="Another picture of a cubby bin with nine different cubby slots. The difference with this cubby bin is that each bin has a label indicating its contents (legos, dolls, trucks, balls, art supplies)." width=50%}


#### Are the vectors all the same length?
<!-- dataframes or tibbles! -->

Technically, a list can have **both** entries with different data types
**and** entries with different lengths. Both `show_name` and `release_year` 
have 4 items. However, there is more than one character in 
each show. We can add these characters to a list in the same way as before:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
characters <- c("Jim", "Weun", "Pat", "Pran", "Phupha", "Black", "Sean")

top_bl <- list(show_name = shows, 
               release_year = release_years, 
               characters = characters)
top_bl
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$show_name
[1] "Tale of a Thousand Stars" "Bad Buddy"               
[3] "Moonlight Chicken"        "Not Me"                  

$release_year
[1] 2021 2021 2023 2022

$characters
[1] "Jim"    "Weun"   "Pat"    "Pran"   "Phupha" "Black"  "Sean"  
```


:::
:::


The list has no problem having entries with different lengths! But, if your
entries all happen to have the **same** length, then you can use a special type
of list---a dataframe. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows_releases <- data.frame(show_name = shows,
                             release_year = release_years)
shows_releases
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                 show_name release_year
1 Tale of a Thousand Stars         2021
2                Bad Buddy         2021
3        Moonlight Chicken         2023
4                   Not Me         2022
```


:::
:::


While a dataframe is a list, the output looks **very** different. Each variable
(`show_name`, `release_year`) is slotted into a column. The numbers on the side
(`1`, `2`, `3`, `4`) represent the element number.

::: {.callout-check-in}

::: {.cell}

:::


Using the output below, identify what type of object you believe was created.

5. What type of object is this?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[[1]]
[1] "Malus domestica"     "Citrus sinensis"     "Citrus limon"       
[4] "Citrus aurantifolia" "Mangifera indica"    "Prunus persica"     

[[2]]
[1]  3  5 12 12  5  3
```


:::
:::


6. What type of object is this?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1] [,2]
[1,]    3   12
[2,]    5    5
[3,]   12    3
```


:::
:::


7. What type of object is this?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  fruits               trees
1  apple     Malus domestica
2 orange     Citrus sinensis
3  lemon        Citrus limon
4   lime Citrus aurantifolia
5  mango    Mangifera indica
6  peach      Prunus persica
```


:::
:::


8. What type of object is this?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$tree
[1] "Malus domestica"     "Citrus sinensis"     "Citrus limon"       
[4] "Citrus aurantifolia" "Mangifera indica"    "Prunus persica"     

$harvest_window
[1]  3  5 12 12  5  3
```


:::
:::


9. What type of object is this?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Malus domestica"     "Citrus sinensis"     "Citrus limon"       
[4] "Citrus aurantifolia" "Mangifera indica"    "Prunus persica"     
```


:::
:::

:::

## Indexing Objects

We will often find ourselves in the position where we are interested in
extracting a component / components of an object. Extracting components from
an object is more frequently called "subsetting," where the subset is a smaller
part of the larger object. Fundamentally, subsetting is done using **indexing**
where the index / indices refer to elements you wish to be kept (or discarded)
from the larger object. 

Let's see how subsetting works on each of the objects we learned about
previously.


<!-- Indexing and subsetting -->
<!-- Accessing elements via []. -->
<!-- Accessing columns in a dataframe / tibble via $. -->
<!-- Accessing elements via T / F. -->

### Vectors

We extract components of a vector using bracketing (`[]`). The value(s) inside 
the brackets indicate what indices should be kept. There are two methods for 
indexing what values should be kept---numbers and logical values. 

#### Indexing with Numbers

Every element of a vector is assigned a number representing its position 
(location) in the vector. R starts counting at `1`^[Other languages, like Python, 
start counting at `0`.]. So, a vector with have indices starting at `1` and 
ending at its last element. 

The vector of `shows` below has 4 elements, so it will have 
indices from `1` up to 4.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", 
           "Bad Buddy", 
           "Moonlight Chicken", 
           "Not Me")
```
:::


If we wanted to extract **a single element** of `shows` we would write
something like `shows[3]`, which would output `"Moonlight Chicken"`. 

Once we want to extract **more than one** element of `shows`, then we need to
make a **vector** containing the indices we want to keep. Remember, we create
a vector using the `c()` function. 

So, if we wanted the first two elements of `shows`, we could write something 
like:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows[c(1, 2)]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Tale of a Thousand Stars" "Bad Buddy"               
```


:::
:::


If we wanted the second and forth elements, we could write something like:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows[c(2, 4)]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Bad Buddy" "Not Me"   
```


:::
:::


::: {.callout-tip}
# Vector Shortcut

If you want to create a vector of consecutive numbers (e.g., `1` through `5`), 
you don't have to type out `c(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)`. There is a shortcut! 

The `:` operator can generate a sequences of numbers. For example, `1:5` would 
generate a sequence of numbers from `1` to `5` (1, 2, 3, 4, 5). So, if you wanted the
first three elements of `shows` you could instead write `shows[1:3]`. 

Much faster!
:::

#### Indexing with Logicals

The second method of subsetting uses logical values (`TRUE`, `FALSE`) instead 
of numbers to grab the desired indices. Each logical value represents whether 
that specific index / element should be retained.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows[c(FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Bad Buddy" "Not Me"   
```


:::
:::


Notice this vector is longer than the previous method (`shows[c(2, 4)]`)! The
vector of logicals must be the **same** length as the original vector, since
each logical value  refers to whether that specific element of the vector should
be kept!

::: {.callout-check-in}

Given this vector, write code to perform the following operations. *Can you find
__multiple__ methods?*


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
fruits <- c("apple",
            "orange", 
            "lemon", 
            "lime", 
            "mango", 
            "peach")
fruits
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "apple"  "orange" "lemon"  "lime"   "mango"  "peach" 
```


:::
:::


10. Grab the first three elements.

11. Grab the first, third, and fifth element.
:::


### Matrices

Where a vector had **one** dimension (length) a matrix has **two**
dimensions---rows and columns. Because we now have a second dimension, there are
two elements in the brackets (`[]`) we use to index. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
name_matrix <- matrix(names, nrow = 2)
name_matrix
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1]   [,2]   [,3]    
[1,] "Jim"  "Pat"  "Phupha"
[2,] "Weun" "Pran" "Tian"  
```


:::
:::


Now when we bracket, we will use something like `[2, 2]`. The first number
refers to the **row** and the second number refers to the **column**. So, 
`[2, 2]` extracts the observation from the second row and second column:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
name_matrix[2, 2]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Pran"
```


:::
:::


If we wanted to grab an entire row we would leave the second value out, thereby
grabbing **every** column:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
name_matrix[2,]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Weun" "Pran" "Tian"
```


:::
:::


If we wanted to grab an entire column we would leave the first value out,
thereby grabbing **every** row:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
name_matrix[,2]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Pat"  "Pran"
```


:::
:::


Grabbing multiple rows / columns works the same way as a what we did with a 
vector, where we need to create a vector of rows / columns we want to keep. 

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
## Rows 1 & 2, Column 2
name_matrix[1:2 , 2]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Pat"  "Pran"
```


:::
:::

:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
## Row 2, Columns 2 & 3
name_matrix[2, 2:3]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Pran" "Tian"
```


:::
:::

:::
:::

::: {.callout-important}
# Vectors everywhere!

Notice that all of the above actions for subsetting a matrix resulted in a 
**vector**. That is because each of these actions isolated a **single** row or a 
**single** column. So, the resulting object only had **one** dimension (not
two). 

We could have gotten a matrix, but we would have needed to isolate **multiple**
rows or **multiple** columns:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
## All Rows, Columns 2 & 3
name_matrix[, 2:3]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1]   [,2]    
[1,] "Pat"  "Phupha"
[2,] "Pran" "Tian"  
```


:::
:::

::: 

::: {.callout-check-in}

Given this matrix, write code to perform the following operations. *Can you find
__multiple__ methods?*


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
trees <- c("Malus domestica", 
           "Citrus sinensis", 
           "Citrus limon", 
           "Citrus aurantifolia", 
           "Mangifera indica", 
           "Prunus persica")

tree_matrix <- matrix(trees, ncol = 2)
tree_matrix
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1]              [,2]                 
[1,] "Malus domestica" "Citrus aurantifolia"
[2,] "Citrus sinensis" "Mangifera indica"   
[3,] "Citrus limon"    "Prunus persica"     
```


:::
:::


12. Grab the tree in the second row, second column.

13. Grab the trees in the first two rows. 

14. Grab the trees in the second column. 
:::


### Lists

Lists differ from matrices and vectors in one key way, the use of `[[]]` to 
isolate a component. Where a matrix used `[3, ]` to isolate the third row, a 
list uses `[[3]]` to isolate the third component. 

Recall the list we made earlier:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
top_bl <- list(show_name = shows, 
               release_year = release_years, 
               characters = characters)

top_bl
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$show_name
[1] "Tale of a Thousand Stars" "Bad Buddy"               
[3] "Moonlight Chicken"        "Not Me"                  

$release_year
[1] 2021 2021 2023 2022

$characters
[1] "Jim"    "Weun"   "Pat"    "Pran"   "Phupha" "Black"  "Sean"  
```


:::
:::


If we wanted to isolate the characters, we could use `[[3]]` to grab that 
component of the list:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
top_bl[[2]]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 2021 2021 2023 2022
```


:::
:::


Alternatively, we could have used a `$` to isolate this component because it has 
a name:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
top_bl$release_year
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 2021 2021 2023 2022
```


:::
:::


Notice that each of these are once again a vector! If we wanted to grab an 
element from either of these vectors, we can use the method we learned 
previously (bracketing). 

<!-- I haven't talked about the third option: [["release_year"]] -->


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
top_bl[[2]][3]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 2023
```


:::
:::


You could probably see how this might get confusing! The `[[]]` and the `[]`
next to each other makes this code a bit difficult to understand. You can,
however, read code like a book---going from left to right. Starting on the left, 
we know the `top_bl[[3]]` grabs the third component from the list `top_bl`. 
We've also learned that this element is a vector. So, when we see the `[3]` we
know that this is extracting the third element of the vector. 

This might take a bit of practice, but you'll get there!

::: {.callout-check-in}

Given this list, write code to perform the following operations. *Can you find
__multiple__ methods?*


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
harvest_window <- c(3, 5, 12, 12, 5, 3)

fruit_tree_harvest <- list(fruit = fruits, 
                           tree = trees, 
                           harvest_window = harvest_window)
fruit_tree_harvest
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$fruit
[1] "apple"  "orange" "lemon"  "lime"   "mango"  "peach" 

$tree
[1] "Malus domestica"     "Citrus sinensis"     "Citrus limon"       
[4] "Citrus aurantifolia" "Mangifera indica"    "Prunus persica"     

$harvest_window
[1]  3  5 12 12  5  3
```


:::
:::


15. Grab the first component (`fruit`).  

16. Grab the third tree from the `tree` component.
:::

### Dataframes

Recall that a dataframe is a special type of list, where every component has the
same length. So, the methods we learned for extracting components from a list
will also work here. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows_releases <- data.frame(show_name = shows,
                             release_year = release_years)
shows_releases
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                 show_name release_year
1 Tale of a Thousand Stars         2021
2                Bad Buddy         2021
3        Moonlight Chicken         2023
4                   Not Me         2022
```


:::
:::


To grab a column from a dataframe you can use `[[]]` or you can use a `$`. We
prefer using a `$`, since the components (columns) of a dataframe are 
**required** to have names.

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows_releases$show_name
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Tale of a Thousand Stars" "Bad Buddy"               
[3] "Moonlight Chicken"        "Not Me"                  
```


:::
:::


:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

<!-- I technically never covered this option, so it might be confusing? -->

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows_releases[["show_name"]]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "Tale of a Thousand Stars" "Bad Buddy"               
[3] "Moonlight Chicken"        "Not Me"                  
```


:::
:::

:::
:::

::: {.callout-note}
# Adding Additional Columns

The `$` operator is also what we can use to add new columns to a dataframe! 
Suppose I want to add a column of the rating information for each show to the
`shows_years` dataframe:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows_releases$rating <- c(9.5, 9.6, 9.1, 9.4)
shows_releases
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                 show_name release_year rating
1 Tale of a Thousand Stars         2021    9.5
2                Bad Buddy         2021    9.6
3        Moonlight Chicken         2023    9.1
4                   Not Me         2022    9.4
```


:::
:::


:::

Interestingly, since a dataframe is made up of vectors with the same length, 
you can also extract components using the notation we learned for matrices. 

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}
**Grab the First Row**

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows_releases[1, ]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                 show_name release_year rating
1 Tale of a Thousand Stars         2021    9.5
```


:::
:::


:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
**Grab the Second Column**

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows_releases[, 2]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 2021 2021 2023 2022
```


:::
:::


:::
:::

::: {.callout-check-in}

Given this dataframe, write code to perform the following operations. *Can you find
__multiple__ methods?*


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
fruit_trees <- data.frame(fruits, trees, harvest_window)
fruit_trees
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  fruits               trees harvest_window
1  apple     Malus domestica              3
2 orange     Citrus sinensis              5
3  lemon        Citrus limon             12
4   lime Citrus aurantifolia             12
5  mango    Mangifera indica              5
6  peach      Prunus persica              3
```


:::
:::


17. Grab the second column (`trees`).

18. Grab the third and forth columns (`trees`, `harvest_window`).

19. Grab the second row of all three columns.
:::

## Mathematical Operations

Now that we know how to extract elements from each of these objects, let's 
learn how to perform mathematical operations! 

Suppose we have a matrix of numerical values:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nums <- c(47, 13, 49, 46, 47, 4, 7, 0, 44, 24, 13, 1)

nums_matrix <- matrix(nums, ncol = 4)
nums_matrix
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4]
[1,]   47   46    7   24
[2,]   13   47    0   13
[3,]   49    4   44    1
```


:::
:::

We could multiply **every** value of `nums_matrix` by `-1` with this simple
code:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nums_matrix * -1
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4]
[1,]  -47  -46   -7  -24
[2,]  -13  -47    0  -13
[3,]  -49   -4  -44   -1
```


:::
:::


Notice how we didn't need to give R specific instructions to multiply each
specific value of `nums_matrix` by `-1`? That is the magic of **vectorized** 
operations---actions that can be done to an entire vector (or matrix)!

Similar to mathematical operations, there are functions that also operate on 
vectors of values. Take the square root function (`sqrt()`):


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sqrt(nums_matrix)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
         [,1]     [,2]     [,3]     [,4]
[1,] 6.855655 6.782330 2.645751 4.898979
[2,] 3.605551 6.855655 0.000000 3.605551
[3,] 7.000000 2.000000 6.633250 1.000000
```


:::
:::

### Operations on Columns & Rows

It is possible that we might want to perform an operation on a row (or column) 
of values and *save the updated values back into the original row (or column)*.
To do this, we have three steps we need to follow: 

1. extract the component(s) of interest
2. perform the mathematical operation
3. save the values back into their original position

Let's see how this might look in the context of `nums_matrix`. Suppose we want
to subtract 30 from all numbers in the first and second rows of the third
column. The first two steps are fairly simple, we extract the first and second rows
with `nums_matrix[1:2, 3]`, and we can do subtraction easily with just a `- 30`. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nums_matrix[1:2, 3] - 30
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] -23 -30
```


:::
:::


The saving back into the original position is the tricky part! We need to 
assign (`<-`) the values we changed back into their original position
(`nums_matrix[1:2, 3]`). So, our code should look like this:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nums_matrix[1:2, 3] <- nums_matrix[1:2, 3] - 30
nums_matrix
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4]
[1,]   47   46  -23   24
[2,]   13   47  -30   13
[3,]   49    4   44    1
```


:::
:::


### Operations with Logical Values

We touched on how we can use logical values to isolate specific components of 
an object. Previously, we created these vectors of logical values ourselves 
(e.g., `c(FALSE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE)`), but this is rarely done in practice. 
Instead, we typically acquire a vector of logical values using a **relational
statement**, a statement which asks how the values of an object relate to a 
specific value (e.g., `40`). 

#### Relational Statements

Let's explore these ideas with a vector of values. We can transform our
`nums_matrix` into a vector using the `c()` function:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nums_vector <- c(nums_matrix)
nums_vector
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 [1]  47  13  49  46  47   4 -23 -30  44  24  13   1
```


:::
:::


Suppose I wanted to know what indices of `nums_vector` were equal to `13`. I 
could write this as a relational statement: `nums_vector == 13`. The output of
this statement is a vector of logical values indicating whether the value of
a particular index satisfied the condition being checked (`== 13`):


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nums_vector == 13
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 [1] FALSE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE  TRUE FALSE
```


:::
:::


::: {.callout-important}
# Why two `=` signs?

Notice that checking *equality* requires **two** `=` signs (`==`). This is 
because a single `=` sign assigns a value into an object (similar to `<-`). 
:::

#### Mathematical Operations with Logical Values

Because logical values have equivalent numerical values (`TRUE` = `1`, 
`FALSE` = `0`), we can perform mathematical operations with these vectors! If 
I wanted to know **how many** values in `nums_vector` were equal to (`== 13`), 
then I could **sum** the resulting vector of logicals:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sum(nums_vector == 13)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 2
```


:::
:::


The output here is 2, which agrees with what we 
saw before. There were 2 values of `TRUE` 
and 10 values of `FALSE`, which added
together gives you a sum of 2. 

We can also use the sums we calculated from these logical values to calculate
**proportions**. Recall that a proportion is the number of instances of an 
event (e.g., `== 13`) out of the total number of possible instances. The total
possible instances is the number of indices in the vector, or the `length()` of
the vector!

Let's find the proportion of numbers in `nums_vector` that are greater than or
equal to 40. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sum(nums_vector >= 40) / length(nums_vector)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 0.4166667
```


:::
:::


::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.callout-note}
# Common Relational Statements

The most commonly used relational statements you will encounter are as follows:

- `==` means "equal to"
- `!=` means "not equal to"
- `>` or `<` means "greater than" or "less than" (note the strict equality)
- `>=` or `<=` means "greater than or equal to" or "less than or equal to"
:::
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.callout-note}
# Common Mathematical Operations

The most commonly used mathematical operations you will encounter are as
follows:

- `mean()` 
- `min()`
- `max()`
- `median()`
- `+`, `-`, `/`, `*`
- `%%` (modulus arithmetic) (e.g., `3 %% 2` = 1)
:::

:::
:::
